skill.name: qa
skill.description: Interactive QA session where user reports bugs or issues conversationally, and the agent files GitHub issues. Explores the codebase in the background for context and domain language. Use when user wants to report bugs, do QA, file issues conversationally, or mentions 'QA session'.
skill.summary: Run interactive QA sessions that turn bug reports into well-written GitHub issues using project domain language.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: This is a documentation-only skill that provides markdown instructions for running QA sessions and filing GitHub issues. The static analyzer flagged 14 patterns, all of which are false positives. The 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' findings (HIGH) are misidentifications of markdown headers and template section names like '## What happened' and '## What I expected'. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings (MEDIUM) are false positives caused by markdown code formatting (e.g., `gh issue create`). No executable code exists in this skill. The only external command reference is the `gh issue create` command, which is a legitimate GitHub CLI operation within the documented workflow.

content.user_title: File GitHub Issues from Bug Reports
content.value_statement: Bug reports get lost or lack context when filed manually. This skill turns conversational QA sessions into durable, well-structured GitHub issues using your project's domain language.
actual_capabilities: Run interactive QA sessions where users describe bugs conversationally
Explore relevant codebase areas in the background for context
Identify and use project domain language from documentation like UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md
File GitHub issues using the `gh issue create` command
Break down complex bug reports into multiple trackable issues
Create issues in dependency order with proper blocking relationships
limitations: Requires `gh` CLI to be installed and authenticated for issue creation
Cannot automatically reproduce bugs or verify fixes
Limited to GitHub as the issue tracking platform
Quality of issues depends on user providing clear reproduction steps

use_cases.title: Product Manager QA Sessions
use_cases.description: Product managers can run structured QA sessions to capture user-reported bugs and convert them into actionable GitHub issues for the development team.

use_cases.title: QA Engineer Bug Triage
use_cases.description: QA engineers can use this skill to standardize how bugs are captured during testing sessions, ensuring consistent issue quality across the team.

use_cases.title: Open Source Maintainers
use_cases.description: Open source maintainers can help contributors file well-structured bug reports by running guided QA sessions that extract the right information.

prompt_templates.title: Start a QA Session
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to run a QA session. I'll describe some issues I'm encountering and I want you to help me file them as GitHub issues.

prompt_templates.title: Report a Single Bug
prompt_templates.prompt: Here's a bug I found: [describe the issue]. Please explore the relevant code area, ask me clarifying questions if needed, and file a GitHub issue.

prompt_templates.title: Report Multiple Related Bugs
prompt_templates.prompt: I found several issues with the checkout flow: [describe issues]. Please file them as separate issues, breaking them down if needed, and create any necessary tracking issue.

prompt_templates.title: Complex Multi-Part Bug
prompt_templates.prompt: There's a complex issue spanning multiple areas. Please help me break it down into separate issues, identify dependencies, and file them in the right order with proper blocking relationships.

output_examples.input: User reports: 'The export to CSV feature is broken. When I click the export button on the dashboard, nothing happens. I expected a CSV file to download.'
output_examples.output: Files GitHub issue with title like 'Export to CSV does nothing when clicked from dashboard' containing: What happened section, What I expected section, Steps to reproduce, and Additional context. Returns the issue URL.

output_examples.input: User reports a complex issue spanning validation, success message, and redirect: 'The form submission has three problems: validation doesn't catch empty fields, the success message never appears, and after submit nothing happens.'
output_examples.output: Breaks down into three separate issues: (1) Form validation accepts empty fields, (2) Success message not displayed after submission, (3) No redirect after form submission. Files them in dependency order with proper blocking relationships.

best_practices: Keep clarifying questions to 2-3 maximum to avoid over-interviewing users
Launch codebase exploration agents in the background while talking to the user to save time
Write issues from the user's perspective using domain language, not internal implementation details
Break complex bugs into multiple thin issues that can be worked on in parallel
anti_patterns: Do not include file paths or line numbers in issues - they go stale after refactors
Do not batch multiple issues - file each one independently for parallel work
Do not ask the user to review issues before filing - file directly and share URLs

faq.question: What is a QA session?
faq.answer: A QA session is an interactive conversation where a user describes bugs or issues they encounter, and the agent explores the codebase to file well-structured GitHub issues.

faq.question: What tools does this skill require?
faq.answer: This skill requires the GitHub CLI (`gh`) to be installed and authenticated for creating issues. It works with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: How does codebase exploration work?
faq.answer: The skill launches an Explore agent in the background while talking to the user, learning domain language and understanding the feature's expected behavior to write better issues.

faq.question: Can this skill fix bugs automatically?
faq.answer: No, this skill only files GitHub issues. It does not attempt to fix bugs or modify code - it focuses on capturing issues in a durable, user-focused format.

faq.question: When should I break down a bug into multiple issues?
faq.answer: Break down bugs when they span multiple independent areas, have separable concerns that could be worked on in parallel, or describe multiple distinct failure modes.

faq.question: Why should issues avoid file paths and line numbers?
faq.answer: File paths and line numbers go stale after refactors. Issues should describe behaviors using domain language so they remain valid and useful long-term.
